sinopse: Project Hail Mary is the kind of movie that reminds you why you love cinema. It's been a long time since I felt such mixed emotions while watching a film-happiness, sadness, love, hope-everything is there. I went into it completely blind. I didn't watch a trailer; I only knew that Ryan Gosling was starring and that it was directed by Phil Lord and Christopher Miller, and that alone made me believe it would be something special.And it really was something special.The direction is fantastic, the visuals are beautiful, and the music is enormous-it melts perfectly into the writing and the story. Everything works together to create something warm, emotional, and incredibly human.At its heart, this is a movie about friendship, loneliness, change, evolution, bravery, and sacrifice. It's about doing good even when the situation feels impossible, and how goodness and courage can come back to you in unexpected ways.In a world that often feels dark and heavy, this film feels like a bright side. It brings genuine joy-the kind of joy movies are supposed to give you.

Forget your Universal Mummy ride...this movie was something from another planet...I'm so glad it was 2 hour because it just kept pushing it to the limit. I don't know if i would watch this movie again because it's alot to take in. Its for hardened horror fans and Will make you feel bad. Doesn't feel like A Blumhouse pg13 horror film at all. The part at the funeral/memorial was so twisted & mean! It feels like Sam Raimi and James Wan made a Evil dead film...It's a mummy curse but it's almost like The Exorcist movie is reborn. Take this as a warning! Leave the kids at home for this one.

Hopefully you will get the opportunity to see this movie without knowing much about it.I had no clue what it was about, my daughter asked me to see it with her. Once it gets going from that certain point it's good.It's uncomfortable, funny and awkward all at the same time. They are both perfect for this role and there is nothing else I would disclose.So please go in this movie blind. Great film.

Walking into Dhurandhar 2 with high expectations and long-built anticipation, I got to experience it exactly the way I had hoped to, and once again, keeping all socio-politics and the obvious, expected talks of propaganda aside, purely from a cinematic storytelling perspective, it delivers, and how.It starts off with the origin tale, spending enough time to keep you invested in the story, before seamlessly transitioning back to the political conflicts of the first part.The use of classic music and sound design remains on point yet again, especially during the high-octane, gory action. Captured beautifully, it delivers just the right amount of mass needed. Beyond the performances, this is what truly stands out as the film's biggest strength.Needless to say, Ranveer Singh is a beast and the epitome of the ideal massy hero in this one, but he delivers equally well in the emotional moments too. A special mention to Arjun Rampal for carrying out his menacing side so effortlessly.The screenplay feels well-balanced, giving each character their moments to shine, with sprinkles of comedic writing that further balance its otherwise bloody tone.With multiple fast-paced twists and montages in the second half, the narrative takes an interesting turn, building up to a slightly long yet gripping climax, with a few cliché one-liners often heard in films following the patriotic template.For someone who walked in with expectations, I would say it has several moments where it exceeds itself and goes beyond them.Those who walk in with a clear, open mind are sure to be met with plenty of surprises, keeping them on the edge of their seats until the very end.All in all, given the hype and excitement surrounding it since the first part, one can easily bet that this is a film with the potential to enjoy a long theatrical run and even break records. So if you are interested, do yourself a favour and watch it on the big screen.It is sure to elevate the experience in ways one cannot imagine.

sinopse: The media reviews of this film are very strange to say the least. Michael the movie puts entertainment first (funny that) and touches on some high profile moments in his development as a man and as an artist.His music and spirit are pushed to the forefront of this picture which is of absolutely no surprise to me.The allegations which main stream critics and media seem to be so dearly missing out on will surely be included in the continuation.Fantastic performances especially from Jaafar and Colman, loved seeing Mike Myers back on the screen in a small cameo.If you're a fan of Michael Jacksons music don't miss out.

The first Super Mario Bros movie hit me so perfectly (9 stars). I wasn't expecting that level with the sequel, but the drop off was still noticeable. I'll try to explain why, but just know that I still had a great time and was smiling for most of the runtime. And I recommend seeing it on ScreenX. More than half the movie uses the side screens.This sequel feels a little empty. The first one is far from deep, but I feel like I had more to latch onto. This one feels like random events taking place. The story feels generally scattered. And I wasn't as invested in the characters.The clearest drop off is the comedy. I laughed about 10 times. The first one had me cracking up regularly, including a few scenes where I was crying from laughter (Peaches song lmao). I would guess it's a combination of weaker writing and fewer attempts.They also seem to be stretching with the references from the different games. I've played like a hundred Mario or Mario-related games. But many of them I haven't played in over a decade. I recognized significantly fewer references than in the first movie.I love Mario. I still want more movies. I just hope they can reach the levels of magic we felt with the first one.(1 viewing, opening Tuesday ScreenX 3/31/2026)

The Housemaid is ridiculously fun, so contrived but ever so entertaining. This in part is helped by the go for broke acting of the leads, especially Seyfried. From the get go it is obvious who the real villain of the story is, but it is the why that keeps the intrigue intact until the reveal. This is a very much throwaway popcorn movie that delivers the goods. In the olden days it would be just the actresses giving the T & A, but now the guys get to show a bit of flesh too ... lol. This isn't any prestige movie, but it provides a fun and entertaining time at the movies.

This movie was honestly really scary, but not in the typical way. It didn't rely on jump scares at all, which made it feel way more intense. Instead of giving you those quick moments of shock and then letting you relax, it just kept this constant tension the entire time.The audio and soundtrack were a huge part of that. There were so many subtle, unsettling sounds that just made everything feel off and uncomfortable. The camera work added to it too. Some shots just lingered longer than you'd expect or were framed in a way that made you feel like something bad was about to happen, even if nothing did right away.The whole movie just felt really unsettling from start to finish. Not just scary, but deeply uncomfortable in a way that gets under your skin. There's this constant feeling that something isn't right, and it never really goes away.What made it stand out to me is that the tension never really got released. You're just sitting there feeling uneasy the whole time, which honestly made it way scarier than most horror movies. It sticks with you after because of that constant pressure instead of just a few big moments.

I just got out of Ready or Not 2: Here They Come.I found the first movie to be really entertaining and enjoyable (was it really 2019??), so I was looking forward to this.Sadly this is nowhere near as good, as the first movie. Which is surprising given it's the same writers, director and main star of the first movie.It's simply a remake of that movie, but not as good and more complicated. I know they were trying to expand on the lore and build on it, but it feels like a Ready or Not rip off, rather than a sequel.Of the cast Samara Weaving is again excellent as Grace!! She alone, is worth seeing this movie for, no phoning it in for the sequel, here. Kathryn Newton is a good addition as Grace's sister Faith, but I very quickly got tired over them repeatedly squabbling about the state of their relationship. Elijah Wood is always reliable,
Sarah Michelle Geller is the biggest stand out of the supporting cast, Shawn Hatosy is solid and I always enjoy seeing Kevin Durand pop up on screen.It was too comedic in tone as the hunters were incompetent to say the least, which led to it losing most of its dramatic tension.I feel like I'm bashing it too much, but I didn't hate it, it has some good, fun and clever moments, but over all it's just a sloppy, convoluted rehash of the first movie, when I was hoping for much better.

Normal had the potential to be an interesting and emotional film, especially with Bob Odenkirk in the cast, but unfortunately it falls short in almost every aspect. While Odenkirk does his best with the material and delivers a solid performance, the script gives him very little to work with. The story feels weak, unfocused, and never fully develops its themes or characters in a meaningful way.The pacing is one of the film's biggest problems. It moves too slowly, with several scenes that feel unnecessary or repetitive, making the overall experience drag. Instead of building emotional depth, the movie often feels flat and disconnected. The attempts at humor and charm also miss the mark, coming across as forced rather than natural.What could have been a thoughtful drama ends up feeling forgettable and lacking impact. Even with a talented actor like Bob Odenkirk involved, Normal never becomes engaging or memorable. In the end, it is a disappointing film that fails to live up to its potential.

sinopse: Send Help quickly establishes itself as one of the most compelling and engaging films of the year, especially for viewers who were already intrigued by its intense and mysterious trailer. From the opening moments, the film pulls you into its twisted emotional landscape, blending suspense, dark humor, and psychological tension. It's the kind of movie that demands your attention and rewards you for staying locked in, creating a theatrical experience that feels both immersive and unpredictable.Dylan O'Brien delivers one of his most striking performances to date, stepping far outside his usual heroic or sympathetic roles. Here, he plays a deeply flawed, abrasive, and often infuriating character, and he commits to it fully. His portrayal makes you uncomfortable in the best way, forcing the audience to confront how selfishness, manipulation, and emotional damage ripple outward. Watching O'Brien lean into this darker, more morally questionable role is both surprising and impressive, proving his range as an actor.Rachel McAdams is the true powerhouse of the film. Her performance is fearless, layered, and wildly dynamic. She shifts seamlessly between vulnerability, humor, instability, charm, and outright madness, often within the same scene. McAdams commands the screen every time she appears, reminding audiences why she remains one of the most versatile actresses working today. Her character is messy, unpredictable, and deeply human, making her journey both disturbing and fascinating to watch.The screenplay is energetic and inventive, blending character-driven drama with moments of sharp intensity. While the story is consistently engaging, it does take a bit too long to reach its emotional breaking point. The buildup to Rachel McAdams' character's major shift is stretched slightly thin, which may test some viewers' patience. However, once that turning point arrives, the film erupts into a powerful and relentless second half that more than makes up for the slow burn.Director Sam Raimi brings his signature style to the project, balancing psychological tension with visual flair and emotional weight. His direction keeps the pacing tight, the atmosphere uneasy, and the performances grounded. Raimi knows when to let scenes breathe and when to unleash chaos, resulting in a film that feels carefully constructed rather than chaotic. His ability to blend intensity with character development is one of the movie's strongest assets.Overall, Send Help stands out as one of the most memorable theatrical releases of the year. With outstanding performances from Dylan O'Brien and Rachel McAdams, a bold screenplay, and confident direction from Sam Raimi, the film delivers a gripping and emotionally charged experience. It's intense, unsettling, and deeply engaging from start to finish. This is not a movie to wait for streaming. It deserves to be seen on the big screen, where its full impact can truly be felt.

I understand many people didn't like the movie. Yes, it was crude and ridiculous, but I watched it after having a few drinks with no expectations-and I found myself laughing the whole way through. I actually enjoyed the unpredictability of the meandering storyline; to me it felt spontaneous, and the absurdity just added to the joke. It was clearly meant to be an absurd comedy, and in that sense it works. You just have to switch off your brain a little and go along for the ride. Suspend disbelief and embrace the absurdity. Leave your critical thinking at the door.

sinopse: In an era often saturated with CGI-heavy spectacle, director Bart Layton's Crime 101 emerges as a refreshingly grounded, character-driven thriller that harks back to the gritty, sun-baked streets of 1990s Los Angeles. Based on Don Winslow's gripping novella, the film follows Mike Davis (Chris Hemsworth), a meticulous jewel thief plotting one final, elaborate score. His carefully calculated operation is soon jeopardized by the arrival of an unpredictable wild card (Barry Keoghan) and the relentless pursuit of Detective Lou Lubesnick (Mark Ruffalo), a seasoned cop whose sharp instincts make him a formidable adversary. Rather than relying solely on explosive set pieces, Layton masterfully anchors the narrative in the psychological chess match between thief and detective, building an atmosphere dripping with palpable tension.What truly elevates Crime 101 from a standard heist procedural into a standout genre entry is its stellar ensemble cast. Hemsworth sheds his usual blockbuster bravado to deliver a restrained, calculating performance that serves as the film's magnetic center. Ruffalo provides a perfect counterbalance as the dogged, world-weary detective, bringing a quiet intensity to their impending collision. The supporting cast is equally sharp, with Keoghan injecting a potent dose of chaotic menace and Halle Berry commanding the screen as an insurance broker who becomes entangled in the web. Layton's direction is taut and confident, utilizing sharp pacing and a neo-noir visual palette to ensure the stakes always feel immediate.While the film occasionally leans into familiar genre tropes, the execution is so sharp and the performances so compelling that these minor blemishes are easily forgiven. Crime 101 is a superlative, old-school cat-and-mouse game that honors the legacy of classic crime cinema without ever feeling like a mere imitation. For audiences craving a meticulously crafted, adult-oriented thriller with real psychological depth and phenomenal acting, this is an undeniable winner. An 8/10.

Thrash is a shark thriller that feels like it's constantly on the verge of becoming something exciting, yet never quite breaks through the surface. From the start, it sets up a survival scenario that should be tense and gripping, but instead of building strong momentum, it drifts along at a slower pace than expected. It's not a terrible film by any means, but it often feels like it's holding itself back from reaching the level of intensity the concept promises.One of the film's biggest strengths is surprisingly the sharks themselves. The CGI is much better than you would expect for a streaming release, and the bull sharks are presented as genuine threats. There's a sense of danger in how they move and attack, which helps elevate certain scenes. However, the inclusion of a great white shark feels like a missed opportunity. It appears in moments that hint at something bigger, but the film never fully commits to giving it the presence or dominance it should have, leaving those scenes feeling underwhelming instead of impactful.Where Thrash really struggles is in its storytelling. The film introduces several characters with potentially interesting backgrounds, including an anxious lead, a mother and child, and a group of survivors with different dynamics. On paper, these elements should create emotional depth and tension, but in execution, they feel more like placeholders. Instead of developing their arcs or giving them meaningful growth, the film uses them to transition between action sequences, making it difficult to truly connect with anyone on screen.The pacing also works against the film. There are moments where it feels like it's building toward something bigger, but those moments rarely pay off in a satisfying way. The narrative structure leans heavily on rescue attempts and survival beats without layering in enough emotional or thematic substance to keep the audience fully invested. As a result, the tension comes and goes rather than steadily building, which weakens the overall impact.Visually and conceptually, the film shows that it had the tools to be more than just another shark movie. There are glimpses of strong direction and creative ideas, particularly in how it stages certain encounters with the sharks. But those moments are not consistent enough to carry the entire film, and they end up feeling like isolated highlights rather than part of a cohesive whole.Ultimately, Thrash is a decent but forgettable entry in the shark thriller genre. It works well enough as a casual streaming watch, especially if you enjoy creature features, but it never rises above mediocrity. The sharks are effective, the setup has potential, but the lack of strong character development and storytelling keeps it from leaving any lasting impression. It has the bones of something better, but in the end, it just never fully takes the bite it needed.
